summary: ripgrep binary detection uses NUL-byte heuristics with different modes for explicit and implicit files.
details: Binary detection is modeled by grep::searcher::BinaryDetection as None, Quit(byte), or Convert(byte). The CLI maps normal automatic behavior to Convert(b'\x00') for explicitly supplied files, because ripgrep should not silently filter a file the user named, and Quit(b'\x00') for files discovered through recursive traversal, where binary files may be skipped early. The active mode is selected per haystack in SearchWorker::search based on haystack.is_explicit(). At runtime, Core::detect_binary scans the relevant buffer/range for the configured byte, records the first binary offset, calls sink.binary_data, and returns whether searching should stop. Fixed-size buffered searches scan filled buffer contents directly. mmap/heap-backed searches are more conservative: Quit checks an initial region and later matching/context lines, while Convert has no effect there.
review_checks: When changing BinaryDetection::Convert, account for the documented mmap/heap caveat: Convert affects fixed-size buffered searches, not mmap-backed whole-content searches.
When changing binary detection defaults, verify both explicit and implicit haystack behavior; explicit paths should not use Quit unless the UX contract changes.
When changing binary detection offsets or warnings, update tests/binary.rs and searcher tests that assert binary offset reporting.

summary: musl builds use jemalloc as the global allocator; glibc and other targets use the system default
details: crates/core/main.rs:38-40 gates a `#[global_allocator]` on `#[cfg(all(target_env = "musl", target_pointer_width = "64"))]`. The reason is that musl's bundled allocator is substantially slower than glibc's, and ripgrep's workload is noticeably affected even though it is not heavily allocation-intensive. jemalloc is intentionally NOT used on non-musl targets because (a) glibc's allocator is already good, and (b) jemalloc increases compile times. The 64-bit guard exists because jemalloc does not support i686. A downstream effect: when running tests under `cross` with QEMU, jemalloc prints spurious `<jemalloc>:` lines to stderr. tests/util.rs:509-513 strips these lines from captured output before assertions.
review_checks: If a test sees unexpected jemalloc lines in stderr during cross/QEMU runs, check strip_jemalloc_nonsense in tests/util.rs.
If adding a new allocator-sensitive code path, confirm it behaves correctly under both glibc and musl targets.

summary: Memory maps are silently disabled on macOS even when MmapChoice::auto() is requested
details: crates/searcher/src/searcher/mmap.rs:73-76 contains an unconditional `if cfg!(target_os = "macos") { return None; }` inside `MmapChoice::open()`. The comment reads 'I guess memory maps on macOS aren't great. Should re-evaluate.' This means that even if the caller explicitly selects `MmapChoice::auto()`, no memory map will ever be created on macOS. The implicit-todos brain flags this file as needing re-evaluation. The practical effect: ripgrep always falls back to buffered I/O on macOS regardless of the `--mmap` flag. Additionally, `MmapChoice::auto()` is an `unsafe fn` because there is no portable way to guarantee that the backing file won't change while the map is live; a SIGBUS is the worst-case consequence.
review_checks: Before re-enabling mmap on macOS, benchmark to confirm it actually helps; the original suppression suggests it was net-negative.
Do not assume --mmap is respected on macOS; the code hard-disables it regardless of the flag.
If adding a new search path that relies on memory maps, test on macOS explicitly — it will silently fall back to buffered I/O.

summary: CLI flags flow through a deliberate three-layer pipeline: defs → LowArgs → HiArgs
details: Adding or changing any CLI flag requires touching at minimum three files in crates/core/flags/: (1) defs.rs — defines the Flag trait impl (name, short name, negation, aliases, docs, update_low_args). The order of entries in the FLAGS slice directly determines the order flags appear in -h, --help, and the man page. (2) lowargs.rs — LowArgs struct holds raw parsed state; each flag's update_low_args() writes into it. (3) hiargs.rs — HiArgs::from_low_args() converts the fully-parsed LowArgs into a richer typed representation (e.g., individual glob strings become a compiled Override matcher). Documentation (doc/help.rs, doc/man.rs) and shell completions (complete/*.rs) are generated from the same Flag trait implementations, so changing a flag description in defs.rs propagates to all of those automatically. Shell completion files are checked in as generated artifacts under crates/core/flags/complete/.
review_checks: Shell completions and man page are generated from Flag impls — run the generation commands after changing flag definitions.
The order of FLAGS in defs.rs is the order flags appear in help output — place new flags in the appropriate alphabetical/category position.
When adding a new flag, touch all three layers: defs.rs (definition + docs), lowargs.rs (storage), hiargs.rs (conversion).

summary: RIPGREP_CONFIG_PATH args are prepended to CLI args, so later CLI flags always override config
details: crates/core/flags/parse.rs:102-103 builds `final_args = config_args + CLI_args`. This means the parser sees config file arguments first and CLI arguments second. Because ripgrep's flag parser uses last-writer-wins semantics for most flags, any CLI flag overwrites the corresponding config value. Concretely: if `.ripgreprc` says `--smart-case` and the user types `--case-sensitive`, the CLI wins. The config file is only read when `RIPGREP_CONFIG_PATH` is set to a non-empty path; the format is one flag per line, comments start with `#`, and values are treated as raw shell arguments (no quoting or expansion). Tests always call `cmd.env_remove("RIPGREP_CONFIG_PATH")` (tests/util.rs:169) to prevent local config files from polluting test results. The logging level is set twice — once after the initial CLI-only parse, and again after the merged parse — so that `--trace` passed on the CLI can capture config file loading messages.
review_checks: Config file format is one argument per line — not shell syntax. Quoting and variable expansion are not supported.
Integration tests must unset RIPGREP_CONFIG_PATH (already done in tests/util.rs Dir::command()) to avoid config pollution.
When debugging unexpected flag behavior, check whether RIPGREP_CONFIG_PATH is set in the environment.

summary: eprintln_locked intentionally locks stdout before writing to stderr to prevent interleaved output
details: crates/core/messages.rs:33-67 defines the `eprintln_locked!` macro. Although it writes to stderr, it first acquires the stdout lock. This is an acknowledged 'abstraction violation' (the comment in the code uses exactly that phrase). The reason: search_parallel uses termcolor, which acquires the stdout lock when writing match lines. If a worker thread tries to print an error to stderr while another thread holds the stdout lock for output, the two streams can interleave on a terminal. By locking stdout first and then writing to stderr, ripgrep ensures error lines are never split across parallel output lines. This is specific to the parallel search path (search_parallel in main.rs). The three message macros build on each other: eprintln_locked (raw output) → message! (gated on MESSAGES flag) → err_message! (sets ERRORED flag + calls message!). The ERRORED global flag is what causes ripgrep to exit with code 2 instead of 0 or 1 when a non-fatal error occurred during search.
review_checks: Any new code that prints to stderr from a parallel context should use eprintln_locked! or err_message!, not eprintln!, to avoid interleaved output.
Exit code 2 means at least one non-fatal error occurred (set via err_message!); exit code 1 means no match found; exit code 0 means match found.

summary: On macOS, TMPDIR is a symlink (/var → /private/var) that breaks gitignore path-prefix matching; tests must canonicalize
details: tests/regression.rs:1659-1705 (test r3179_global_gitignore_cwd) documents this precisely. On macOS, TMPDIR is set to /var/folders/... but /var is a symlink to /private/var. The CWD reported by the process is /private/var/..., while dir.path() returns /var/... — these have different string representations so the CWD is not a proper prefix of the test directory path, which causes gitignore pattern stripping to fail. The test works around this by calling dir.path().canonicalize() to resolve the symlink before passing the path to ripgrep. The proper fix would be for the ignore crate's gitignore matching to be relative to the WalkBuilder's root path rather than the process CWD — but this is acknowledged as a large architectural change requiring a rewrite of the ignore crate. Windows has the opposite problem: path canonicalization on Windows produces UNC paths (\\?\...) that break things differently, so the test skips canonicalization on Windows.
review_checks: Do not assume dir.path() and process::getcwd() agree on macOS — /var is a symlink to /private/var.
Do not canonicalize on Windows — it produces UNC paths (//?/...) that break path comparisons differently.
Tests that pass --ignore-file with an absolute path and also rely on CWD-relative matching must canonicalize dir.path() on macOS.

summary: mmap auto-mode only activates for ≤10 explicit file paths; threads are capped at 12 and sorting forces single-threaded
details: Two non-obvious heuristics in crates/core/flags/hiargs.rs:168-249. (1) Thread count: ripgrep defaults to min(available_parallelism, 12). The cap of 12 is hardcoded. --sort or --sort-files forces threads=1 because sorted output requires sequential processing. A single explicit file path also forces threads=1 (paths.is_one_file). (2) mmap auto-mode (MmapMode::Auto): memory maps are only attempted when the number of explicit paths is ≤10 AND all of them are confirmed to be files (not directories). If the user searches a directory tree, mmap is never used even in auto mode. The comment in the code explicitly flags the safety reasoning as 'suspect' (hiargs.rs:231). Additionally, mmap is separately hard-disabled on macOS in the searcher layer (a separate lesson covers this). Binary detection behavior also differs between mmap and buffered paths: in buffered mode it scans the whole buffer; in mmap mode it only checks a small header plus match lines.
review_checks: --sort implicitly sets threads=1; any feature depending on parallelism will be disabled when sorting is active.
Binary detection behavior differs between mmap and buffered search paths — test both when modifying binary handling.
Do not expect mmap to be used when searching directories — auto-mode only activates for ≤10 explicit file arguments.
Thread count is capped at 12 even on many-core systems; do not assume available_parallelism is the actual thread count.

summary: PCRE2 multiline look-ahead requires a kludge: the printer searches 128 bytes past each match boundary
details: crates/printer/src/lib.rs:78-87 defines `const MAX_LOOK_AHEAD: usize = 128`. PCRE2 cannot correctly search a substring of its input when the pattern contains a look-ahead — it needs to see what comes after the candidate match. The grep-printer crate works around this by extending every search region by 128 bytes beyond the nominal match end, letting PCRE2 peek ahead. This kludge is only active in multiline mode. The code comments note that the proper fix would be to refactor the grep crate interfaces so they can communicate look-ahead requirements to the searcher, but that is described as 'a large change'. crates/printer/src/util.rs references the same kludge (find_iter_at_in_context) and explicitly marks the grep interface as needing a refactor to pass matches through properly. If this constant ever needs to be changed, it lives in crates/printer/src/lib.rs and must be coordinated with how search regions are computed in crates/printer/src/util.rs and crates/printer/src/standard.rs.
review_checks: Any refactor of the printer's find_iter_at_in_context must account for the PCRE2 look-ahead extension.
If look-ahead patterns produce incorrect results in multiline mode, check MAX_LOOK_AHEAD in crates/printer/src/lib.rs — 128 bytes may not be sufficient for all patterns.
This kludge only applies to PCRE2 multiline mode; the default regex engine does not have this limitation.

summary: Cross-compilation tests require a manual QEMU binary name mapping because CROSS_RUNNER always points to a non-existent qemu-user
details: tests/util.rs:459-499 documents the pain. When running under the `cross` tool (cross-compilation CI), the `CROSS_RUNNER` env var is supposed to name the QEMU binary to use for executing the target binary. In practice, `cross` always sets it to `qemu-user`, which does not exist in the Docker image. The actual QEMU binary names follow the pattern `qemu-<arch>` but do not match Rust's target architecture naming — e.g. powerpc64 maps to `qemu-ppc64`, not `qemu-powerpc64`. The cross_runner() function in tests/util.rs manually handles these mismatches, with a fallback to `qemu-{ARCH}` for unknown architectures. A related helper is_cross() checks whether cross/QEMU is active; some tests are skipped under cross (via is_cross() guards) because they are too brittle to pass under QEMU emulation. The jemalloc stderr noise under QEMU is a separate issue handled by strip_jemalloc_nonsense().
review_checks: If a new integration test fails only under CI cross-compilation, check whether is_cross() needs to be used to skip or adapt it.
If adding support for a new target architecture, update the cross_runner() match in tests/util.rs with the correct qemu binary name.
jemalloc stderr noise under QEMU is filtered by strip_jemalloc_nonsense(); do not add new stderr assertions without accounting for this.

summary: gitignore.rs uses deprecated std::env::home_dir and a regex-based INI parser instead of a real git config parser
details: crates/ignore/src/gitignore.rs has two intentional shortcuts. (1) home_dir() at line 663 calls `std::env::home_dir()` with `#![allow(deprecated)]`. The comment acknowledges it is deprecated but accepts its known bugs as 'minor corner cases'. The function is used in three places: reading ~/.gitconfig, ~/.config/git/config, and expanding ~ in excludesFile paths. (2) parse_excludes_file() at line 628 uses a regex (`(?im-u)^\s*excludesfile\s*=\s*"?\s*(\S+?)\s*"?\s*$`) to extract the core.excludesFile setting from git config. The code comment explicitly says 'This is the lazy approach, and isn't technically correct, but probably works in more circumstances. I guess we would ideally have a full INI parser.' This means edge cases in git config syntax (multi-value keys, conditional includes, quoted values with spaces) are silently mishandled. The function is only called approximately once per traversal, so performance is not a concern.
review_checks: If a user's core.excludesFile is not being picked up, the regex parser is the first place to check.
git config features like [includeIf] conditional blocks, multi-value keys, and quoted values with spaces are NOT handled — only simple `excludesFile = path` lines work.
home_dir() uses the deprecated std::env::home_dir; if Rust ever removes it, replace with the `home` crate or read $HOME directly.

summary: Before adding a new flag to surface skipped-file feedback, route through the existing ignore_message! system instead
details: ripgrep has a built-in three-tier messaging system in crates/core/messages.rs: message! (informational), err_message! (informational + sets exit code 2), and ignore_message! (ignore/filter-rule specific). All three are gated by the MESSAGES and IGNORE_MESSAGES atomics, which are toggled by --messages / --no-messages. This means users already have a single knob for suppressing noisy filter feedback. The --max-filesize skip in crates/ignore/src/walk.rs:1927-1933 currently only calls log::debug!(), which is invisible unless --debug is passed. This means files silently disappear from results with no user-visible indication. The correct fix is NOT to add a --max-filesize-warning flag — that would be a one-off solution that bypasses the existing system. The right approach is to route the skip notification through ignore_message!() so it participates in the --messages / --no-messages toggle that users already know. The same principle applies to any other filter path (--max-depth, --max-count, type filters, glob filters) that currently only logs at debug level: check whether the skip feedback belongs in ignore_message! before reaching for a new flag.
review_checks: Any filter skip that currently only calls log::debug!() is a candidate for promotion to ignore_message!() — not a new flag.
Before adding a new flag to expose skipped-file or filtered-file feedback, check whether ignore_message! in crates/core/messages.rs is the right vehicle instead.
ignore_message! is already gated by --no-messages, so users can suppress it without any additional flag design work.
